Summary of Chapter 1: Prologue: A Bio-Bibliographical Introduction From The Mage’s Images: Heinrich Khunrath in His Oratory and Laboratory, Volume 1 by Peter J. Forshaw Overview: This chapter presents a detailed bio-bibliographical account of Heinrich Khunrath (1560–1605), a Christian theosophist, alchemist, and Hermetic philosopher. Forshaw traces Khunrath’s life, education, professional career, major publications, and intellectual context, highlighting his key role in the fusion of Paracelsian alchemy, Christian Cabala, and magical philosophy in the late 16th century. Key Biographical Milestones: 1. 1560 – Birth in Leipzig Born into a Lutheran family in Leipzig, Heinrich Khunrath was immersed early in the religious and intellectual ferment of Reformation Germany. 2. 1570 – Matriculation at University of Leipzig Khunrath began his formal education here, likely engaging with theology and natural philosophy. 3. 1575 – Apprenticeship in Alchemy His interest in alchemy likely developed at this time, setting him on a lifelong path of esoteric exploration. 4. 1588 – Medical Education in Basel Khunrath earned his medical degree from the University of Basel with a pro-Paracelsian thesis, De Signatura Rerum Naturalium , defending Paracelsian natural philosophy. 5. Late 1580s – Early Career Khunrath met key intellectual figures, including a legendary (if possibly mythologized) meeting with John Dee in Bremen in 1589, reinforcing his orientation toward mystical alchemy. 6. 1591 – Patronage from Vilém Rožmberk As court physician to this influential Bohemian nobleman, Khunrath gained both financial stability and political protection to pursue his spiritual and scientific work. 7. 1592–1597 – Prague and Magdeburg Periods Khunrath wrote and edited many of his major works in these years. He also developed friendships with other Hermetic thinkers and critics. Major Works and Intellectual Themes: 1595 – First Edition of Amphitheatrum Sapientiae Aeternae Printed in Hamburg, this is his magnum opus—a blend of alchemy, Christian mysticism, and philosophy of nature. It combined text and elaborate engravings to convey esoteric ideas. 1596–1599 – Chaos and Physico-Chemical Writings Works like Confessio de Chao Physico-Chemicorum and Universal Magnesia of the Philosophers explored alchemical theories of matter and the philosopher's stone. 1597 – Magical Armor Manuscript Consilium de Vulcani magica fabrefactione armorum Achillis imagined a magical forging of armor, drawing inspiration from classical mythology and magical theory. 1602 – Second Edition Enhancements New engravings were prepared for a later edition of Amphitheatre . Khunrath’s symbolic and visual thinking was central to his project, aiming to integrate oratory (prayer/contemplation) with laboratory (practice/experimentation). Philosophical Identity: Khunrath positioned himself at the intersection of three streams: Alchemy (Alchymia) : Practical and spiritual transformation. Magic (Magia) : Based on natural and divine correspondences. Cabala (Kabala) : Mystical exegesis of Scripture and cosmic structure. These three domains are emphasized in his personal emblem, writings, and his 1602 portrait, which Forshaw examines in detail. The image shows Khunrath framed by books and instruments, bridging scholarly and practical disciplines. Naming and Self-Image: Pseudonym "Thrasybulus" : A Greek name meaning "bold counsel," a clever translation of his German name (Kühn-rath). Anagram "Ricenus" : From "Henricus." These pseudonyms reflect Khunrath’s alignment with humanist naming traditions and his desire to cloak his identity in mystery. Relationship to Contemporaries and Critics: Andreas Libavius : One of Khunrath’s fiercest critics, who saw his work as mystical nonsense rather than rigorous science. Paracelsus and Dee : Spiritual and philosophical forebears, whose blend of medicine, magic, and theology influenced Khunrath deeply. Scholarly Significance: Forshaw situates Khunrath within the broader tradition of Christian theosophy, arguing that Khunrath is a key transitional figure between medieval esotericism and early modern science. His synthesis of visual and textual media prefigures Rosicrucian symbolic philosophy and the Hermetic revival. Chapter 2 Summary: Images in the Amphitheatre From The Mage’s Images: Heinrich Khunrath in His Oratory and Laboratory, Volume 1 by Peter J. Forshaw (Brill, 2024) Pages 123–334 Overview This chapter is a rich and detailed exploration of the Amphitheatrum Sapientiae Aeternae as a visual, alchemical, and theosophical artifact. Forshaw treats the engraved images of Khunrath’s work not as illustrations but as essential components of his philosophical message. The images are analyzed within the tradition of Renaissance emblems, hieroglyphica, and the intersection of magic, Christian Cabala, and early modern science. Part I: The Image of an Amphitheatre The chapter opens by explaining how the term “amphitheatre” itself is symbolic. Rather than a literal structure, it is a metaphor for the totality of wisdom, synthesized through image, text, symbol, and ritual (pp. 123–125). Forshaw examines Khunrath’s use of sacred architecture and geometrical symbolism, arguing that the engravings function as initiatory gateways into spiritual and alchemical truths. Part II: Sculpta, Picta, Scripta – ‘Not Just of Words … but Rather of Things’ A key section explains Khunrath's integration of visual and verbal modes of knowledge. The Latin phrase emphasizes that Khunrath’s project is based on res (things) rather than mere verba (words) (p. 126). Khunrath aligns himself with the Hermetic tradition where symbolic images act as conveyors of occult truths—truths inaccessible through language alone. Part III: The Amphitheatre Engravings The "Twelve" Core Images (pp. 127–334): 1. Title Page - Laden with theosophical and apotropaic phrases (e.g., “Hallelu-Jah!,” “Fiat!,” “Phi diabolo!”). - Typography emphasizes Christian Cabala, Divine Magic, and Physico-Chemistry. 2. Khunrath’s Portrait - Shows him surrounded by books and instruments, reinforcing his trinitarian integration of Oratory, Laboratory, and Library. 3. School of Nature (Gymnasium Naturae) - Depicts nature as teacher; filled with alchemical symbols and landscapes (p. 150–151). - Connected with Paracelsian pedagogy and the idea of nature as a living manuscript. 4. Pyramid (Designatio Pyramidum) - A symbolic structure with cosmic and geometrical associations; evokes the Emerald Tablet and the Hermetic tradition (p. 152). 5. Alchemical Citadel (Hypotyposis Arcis) - A fortified temple of wisdom with multiple levels, alluding to both initiatory stages and the structure of the universe (p. 155). - Contains a “golden fleece” hidden within, representing esoteric gnosis. 6. Entrance to the Amphitheatre (Porta Amphitheatri) - A visual threshold guarded by the symbolic Sun and Moon on twin obelisks; initiation is dependent on understanding “matter” and its preparation (p. 285)【12:12†source】. 7. Calumniators - Possibly representing Khunrath’s critics or worldly temptations; an image of the dangers awaiting the careless seeker. 8. Circular Figure 1: Christ-Cruciform / Seal of God - Interpreted by Forshaw and others (e.g., Schmidt-Biggemann) as Theogony —divine origin (p. 171). - At the center is Christ, symbolizing divine unity and the culmination of spiritual ascent. 9. Circular Figure 2: Adam-Androgyne - Seen as Cosmogony : the creation of the world and humanity. - Portrays a dual-natured Adam, embodying the reconciliation of opposites (p. 172)【12:6†source】. 10. Circular Figure 3: Rebis / Alchemical Hermaphrodite - An alchemical emblem of perfected matter—both male and female, solar and lunar (pp. 173–175). - Directly parallels the Christian-Cabalist androgynous Adam of Figure 2, connecting microcosm to macrocosm. 11. Circular Figure 4: Oratory–Laboratory - A detailed interior depicting the spiritual and practical spaces of transformation (pp. 204–206). - Divided into the Oratorium (the space of prayer, mysticism, and Cabala) and the Laboratorium (the physical workshop). - A table with open books (including the Biblia , turned to Psalm 145:19) bridges sacred scripture with esoteric science. 12. The Owl - Represents wisdom, night-vision, and possibly the hidden side of alchemical study. Interpretation and Context Forshaw compares the structure and sequence of the images across multiple copies of the Amphitheatrum (e.g., 1595 Hamburg, 1608, 1609 Hanau), noting variation in order and inclusion (pp. 156–157). Comparative tables align interpretations from scholars like Umberto Eco, Töllner, and Schmidt-Biggemann, showing different naming conventions and emphases (p. 156)【12:9†source】. Conclusion of Chapter 2 The engravings in Khunrath’s Amphitheatrum are not mere ornamentation but a visionary system of esoteric theology and laboratory metaphysics. They dramatize the alchemist’s journey as both material and spiritual, blending devotional Christian practice with Paracelsian chemical theory. Forshaw sets the stage for deeper exploration of the meanings and sources of each engraving in the following chapters. Chapter 3 Summary: “Summa Amphitheatri and the Table of the Three Things” (from Peter J. Forshaw, The Mage’s Images: Heinrich Khunrath in His Oratory and Laboratory , Vol. 1 ) Chapter 3 explores two of the most visually and conceptually central double-page tables from the 1609 edition of Heinrich Khunrath’s Amphitheatrum Sapientiae Aeternae : the Summa Amphitheatri and Three Things that Primordially Constitute the World . These tables were not part of the 1595 edition and represent a culmination and structural overview of Khunrath’s theosophical-alchemical system. 1. Summa Amphitheatri: “Panta Lithon Kinei” Title Meaning: The phrase “Panta Lithon Kinei” is a Hermetic motto meaning “All [Things] Move the Stone” or “The Stone Moves All Things” — a reversal of “Panta Rhei” (“Everything flows”), nodding to Heraclitus but transformed through alchemical philosophy. Purpose: This table functions as an abstract of the Amphitheatre 's entire argument and purpose. It synthesizes Khunrath’s complex integration of Christian Cabala, Divine Magic, and Physico-Chemical practice into a single, systematizing diagram. Structure: The Summa is laid out in four quadrants, each filled with symbols and Latin texts referring to the Oratory (Divine Wisdom) and the Laboratory (Alchemical Praxis), thus reflecting the dual path of spiritual and physical refinement. References: Forshaw highlights the positioning of Christ and Wisdom at the top of the table, reflecting the image of Sophia and divine order reigning above the work of the philosophers【16:0†source】. 2. Table of the Three Primordial Things: “Tria Sunt Quae Primordialiter Constituunt Mundum” Theme: This table outlines the three principles that, according to Khunrath, fundamentally constitute the cosmos: - Spiritus (Spirit), - Anima (Soul), - Corpus (Body). Source: This tripartite division is drawn from both Hermetic and Paracelsian frameworks, particularly aligning with the tria prima (Salt, Sulphur, Mercury) but reinterpreted in a more explicitly spiritual and theosophical lens. Visual Layout: The table positions these three aspects in a triadic geometric arrangement, stressing their dynamic interplay and unity. Significance: The emphasis on “primordial” highlights Khunrath’s belief that these principles are not only metaphysical abstractions but practical guides to the alchemist’s task of perfecting nature and the self. 3. Print History and Insertion Issues These tables were inconsistently placed in various editions of the Amphitheatre . Forshaw notes: - In some copies, the Summa Amphitheatri appears between pp. 18–19. - The Three Things… table appears variously after Grade 5, Grade 6, or even before the Imperial Privilege in some rare versions【16:1†source】. 4. Hermetic-Christian Symbolism Both tables combine: - Christian Theology: Christ as the cornerstone of Wisdom, with scriptural citations throughout. - Cabala: Reuchlinian and Lullist elements, especially the symbolism of divine names, geometric forms, and sephirothic structure. - Alchemy: Paracelsian matter theory, especially the importance of Magnesia and the concept of a Philosophers’ Stone which bridges spiritual and material realms. 5. Function in the Work The tables are meant as keys to interpreting the rest of the Amphitheatre, much like Lull’s Ars Magna wheels or alchemical mandalas. Wisdom is not granted to the impure; only those with a cleansed heart and God-fearing humility may proceed (pp. 94–96). It moves beyond the introductory ethical and biblical commentaries of earlier grades and demands that the reader become a vessel capable of receiving divine wisdom through a radical transformation of soul and perception. This Grade builds upon the prior spiritual-ethical foundations and deepens the discussion of divine wisdom , alchemical labor , and mystical illumination . It is the penultimate Grade, acting as a threshold to the visionary culmination found in the final section. Major Themes 1. Refinement through Trials The soul must endure tests, tribulations, and symbolic fire to become worthy of wisdom. Khunrath emphasizes how divine discipline tempers the philosopher's mind, just as metal is purified in a furnace (pp. 145–147). These trials serve not to punish, but to prepare the adept for divine gifts—a Gnostic motif refigured through Lutheran theosophy and Paracelsian alchemy. 2. Divine Love and Philosophic Eros Khunrath increasingly speaks of divine love not merely as a theological virtue but as a magnet for divine wisdom . Wisdom is personified as the beloved Sophia, whom only the spiritually and emotionally purified seeker can wed (pp. 148–150). This theme draws on Neoplatonic eros mysticism, echoing the Song of Songs, and is encoded through cabalistic numerology and image-syllogisms. 3. Active and Contemplative Life (Martha and Mary) A central tension explored is that between labora (work) and ora (prayer) —alchemical labor and divine contemplation. Khunrath insists the true magus must integrate both the active life of Martha and the contemplative life of Mary (p. 153). Forshaw connects this to the famous image of the Oratorium et Laboratorium , where the mystical and material merge into one alchemical-theosophical practice. Esoteric Techniques 4. Mnemonic and Meditative Tools Verses are not merely read but internalized through meditation, visualization, and incantation. Marginal glosses include abbreviations, sigils, and complex marginalia , likely meant for use in prayer-rituals or as focal points in meditative work (pp. 155–157). 5. Kabbalistic Permutations and Magical Names The Sixth Grade contains one of the densest clusters of divine name permutations , exploring variations of the Tetragrammaton, IESU, and permutations linking Christ, Solomon, and Hermes Trismegistus . These permutations are interpreted theologically, magically, and even numerologically , building a web of associations intended to reprogram the initiate’s perception of divine order (pp. 158–161). Metaphysical Geometry and Cosmology Khunrath’s commentary reveals a metaphysical structure to reality based on three-fold and four-fold patterns , reflecting the alchemical tetractys and Christian Trinitarianism. Forshaw identifies links to Lullian combinatorics and the microcosm-macrocosm framework, drawing from Agrippa and Paracelsus (pp. 162–165). Transition to the Culminating Grade The Sixth Grade serves as a kind of hermetic novitiate's gateway to the full vision to come. It prepares the seeker to “see with open eyes,” echoing Revelation’s promise to those who overcome. Forshaw stresses how this Grade, while esoterically dense, is a practical manual of inner transformation , demanding personal discipline and mystical openness (pp. 170–172). Conclusion The Sixth Grade is both an intensification and synthesis of the prior teachings. It integrates ethics, love, labor, prayer, geometry, magic, and cabala into a comprehensive spiritual praxis. Forshaw’s commentary on this chapter reveals how Khunrath aimed to train the initiate to hold together disciplinary rigor and divine intimacy , preparing the soul for the imminent arrival of the Divine Light in the Seventh Grade. Forshaw, _The Mage’s Images_, Vol. 1 , pp. 173–181) This final grade of Khunrath’s “Amphitheatre of Eternal Wisdom” serves as a spiritual and literary culmination of the entire seven-stage process—both alchemical and contemplative. The Seventh Grade focuses on the consummation of the adept's journey: divine union, the Beatific Vision, and the philosopher’s total transformation. Major Themes 1. Consummation in the Divine Light This grade is suffused with illumination metaphors , especially divine light and fire, denoting not only enlightenment but also mystical union (pp. 173–174). The adept has endured trials (Grades 1–6), and now stands purified before divine wisdom. This evokes Christian apophatic mysticism (e.g., Dionysius the Areopagite), Paracelsian light-doctrine, and Kabbalistic _Ein Sof_. 2. The Wedding of the Soul and Sophia Forshaw identifies a “ sacred nuptial imagery ” recurring throughout the 7th Grade, with references to the Song of Songs and Wisdom literature portraying Sophia as the bride of the illuminated soul (pp. 174–176). The verse commentary integrates Lutheran theology (faith, grace, regeneration) with esoteric spiritual alchemy. 3. Christic and Solomonic Confluence Christ is not merely Savior but also Logos and Solomonic Philosopher-King , guiding the magus toward heavenly gnosis. Khunrath blends New Testament Christology with Old Testament wisdom (especially Proverbs, Ecclesiastes, and Wisdom of Solomon), culminating in a paradoxically humble and exalted divine-human union. Esoteric Features and Devices 4. Hieroglyphic Theology Many verses contain symbolic words and syllables that Forshaw suggests are meant to be read meditatively or even vocally invoked , like names of power (pp. 176–177). Visual cues, marginal signs, and abbreviations further layer this esoteric reading, encouraging the adept to see behind the letter to the spiritual image. 5. Hermetic-Cabalistic Synthesis The final verses synthesize Khunrath’s entire project: hermetic correspondences , cabalistic exegesis , and alchemy as sacred art . Emphasis is placed on the microcosmic transformation of the human (anthropos) into a divine vessel, echoing ideas from the _Corpus Hermeticum_ and _Zohar_ alike. Closure and Esoteric Vision Verse 365—symbolizing completion and fullness —draws a perfect circle around the work. It represents both a temporal cycle (365 days) and a spiritual one : the soul’s journey from worldly ignorance to divine union (pp. 180–181). Forshaw observes that Khunrath’s system is not a closed scholastic logic but a living praxis , open to experiential transformation . The prose commentary ends not with dogma, but with invitation —to begin the alchemical journey anew, now informed by divine light. Overall Function The Seventh Grade is a capstone, both mystical and didactic. It delivers: A vision of the divine promised in prior grades. A formal closure to the scriptural meditations. An esoteric map for recursive spiritual practice: a circular rather than linear model of gnosis. Khunrath’s alchemical theology here reaches a level that is profoundly participatory , demanding that the reader not merely understand, but be transformed . Key Themes and Elements 1. Praise and Gratitude to the Divine Khunrath opens with a burst of praise for God, especially Christ the Logos, describing Him as the true source of all wisdom and the light that illuminates the sacred philosophical work. He emphasizes the tripartite authorship of the work: Divine inspiration , the Holy Scriptures , and his own experience as a Christian philosopher-theosopher . He expresses profound gratitude that God has allowed him to survive the labor of completing this great work and to fulfill his theurgical and philosophical calling【40:0†source】. 2. Khunrath’s Vision of the Amphitheatre He makes clear that the Amphitheatre is not a conventional book. It is meant to be: A spiritual journey —not just for the intellect but for the soul. A manual for divine transformation through contemplative theosophy and alchemical purification. A theatre of divine truth , where the seeker witnesses sacred mysteries unfold. Khunrath calls the text “divine magic” and “true natural theology” , resisting accusations of heresy by anchoring his interpretations in Scripture and orthodox Christian piety. 3. Esoteric Warning and Appeal to the Reader He warns that this book is not for the many , echoing his phrase “e millibus vix uni” (“from thousands, scarcely one [understands]”). Like a Rosicrucian manifesto avant la lettre, he calls for wisdom seekers and true lovers of divine philosophy , not idle speculators or mere book collectors. This passage echoes warnings in the works of Trithemius and Reuchlin about the rarity and difficulty of true spiritual gnosis. 4. Emphasis on Christ and Sophia Christ remains the central figure: not only as Savior, but as Sophia (Wisdom incarnate) and the philosopher-king who rules over the sacred theatre. Khunrath speaks of spiritual marriage and the mystical union between the purified soul and divine wisdom. There are strong affinities here with: Johann Arndt’s idea of mystical regeneration, Valentin Weigel’s notion of inner illumination, and Paracelsian spiritual alchemy as rebirth in the spirit. 5. Khunrath’s Tone: A Mix of Humility and Urgency Despite the grandeur of his vision, Khunrath adopts a humble tone , often comparing himself to an unworthy vessel or servant of God. Yet he is urgent and insistent : the Amphitheatre is a gift and a call to action. He charges his reader to take the work seriously, approach it prayerfully, and enact its teachings in life and laboratory. Function of the Colophon This Colophon is more than a signature; it serves: As a seal on the sevenfold path of initiation. As a frame for the unfolding visual and hermeneutic mysteries to come in the Isagoges and Figures. As a witness to the transformative potential of alchemy, Scripture, and mystical union. Khunrath thus blends Lutheran theology, Christian Cabala, Hermetic vision, and spiritual alchemy into a final appeal to wisdom seekers to follow the path he has illuminated. Defending the Use of Images Khunrath defends the use of sacred images, citing: Scripture (particularly Old Testament references to visions and symbols). Church Fathers and mystical authorities. Integration of Magic, Cabala, and Theology This Isagoge exemplifies Khunrath’s Christian Theosophical method , blending: The Magical Word (Logos), The Christian Cabala (especially Reuchlin and Pico’s influence), The natural symmetries of alchemy. Peter Forshaw’s The Mage’s Images: Heinrich Khunrath in His Oratory and Laboratory, Volume 2 offers a detailed exploration of Khunrath’s engagement with key figures in Christian Cabalism and Hermetic thought—particularly Johannes Reuchlin, Heinrich Cornelius Agrippa, and John Dee. Below is a summary of Khunrath’s intellectual relationship with these figures, supported by direct quotations and page references from the book. 1. Johannes Reuchlin (1455–1522) Reuchlin was central to Khunrath’s understanding of Christian Cabala. Forshaw notes that De Arte Cabalistica and De Verbo Mirifico were primary sources for Khunrath’s cabalistic theory. He even paraphrased Reuchlin’s De triplici ratione cognoscendi Deum in his Amphitheatre without acknowledgment【102:4†source】. Forshaw writes: “Unacknowledged verbatim borrowings and obvious modifications from Reuchlin’s De Arte can be found on Amph.II, 4, 10, 65, 89, 95, 109” and from De Verbo Mirifico on “Amph.II, 90, 102, 124, 131, 171, 172, 209, 216”【102:6†source】. Khunrath admired Reuchlin for his “divine expertise in Cabalistic exercises” and referred to him as “doctissimus Capnio (cuius ob Cabalisticæ exercitationis diuinam peritiam memoria sit sempiterna)”【102:6†source】. Reuchlin’s triple synthesis of Christianity, Pythagoreanism, and Cabala is foundational to the Amphitheatre , especially in the symbolic structure of the Tetraktys : “To a contemporary reader, the influence of Reuchlin’s ‘triple identification’ of Pythagoreanism, Cabala and Christianity must have been immediately apparent from the top of the 1609 Amphitheatre ’s title-page…”【102:6†source】. 2. Heinrich Cornelius Agrippa (1486–1535) Agrippa’s Three Books of Occult Philosophy was one of the most influential texts for Khunrath. Forshaw discusses how Agrippa, though not deeply versed in Hebrew or Aramaic, brought together “Pythagorean, Neoplatonic and kabbalistic ideas” and deeply shaped Christian Cabalist discourse【102:10†source】. Forshaw points out: “Scholem considers the place of honour in De Occulta Philosophia is accorded to practical Kabbalah and arithmology, for Book 2 is a rich source of information on the occult and kabbalistic significance of numbers”【102:10†source】. In Amph.II, 30 , Khunrath includes Agrippa among the most learned of the age: “Reuchlinus, Erasmus, Lutherus, H.C. Agrippa, Philippus Theophrastus Paracelsus, Vigelius”【102:6†source】. Agrippa’s symbolic theology is echoed in Khunrath’s use of “letters, numbers, figures...as significative of the most profound matters”【102:15†source】. 3. John Dee (1527–1608/9) Dee had a direct personal relationship with Khunrath, which Forshaw describes: “Dee is known to have been visited by Khunrath in Bremen in 1589 and it was doubtless the English magus who put Khunrath in contact with his former patron in Bohemia”【102:1†source】. Khunrath dedicated his Quaestiones Tres to Dee: “Ad Magnificos Doctissimos et Celeberrimos Viros Dn. Ioannem Dee, Londiniensem…”【102:14†source】. Dee’s Monas Hieroglyphica was a major influence. Khunrath appreciated Dee’s “Cabala of the Real,” where the Book of Nature becomes a medium of divine revelation—an idea Forshaw emphasizes as pivotal to Khunrath’s thought: “Like John Dee, [Khunrath] promotes...the notion of a ‘Cabala of the Real’, new readings of the Book of Nature”【102:7†source】. Dee’s use of Kabbalistic techniques such as Tziruph and his belief in the Shemoth as divine names (e.g., “Let the Will of Jehovah Zebaoth be done…”) are echoed in Khunrath’s mystical theology【102:0†source】【102:5†source】. Forshaw notes that: “In the Amphitheatre ’s first circular figure, Khunrath provides a highly original image of how a Christian Cabalist could adopt and adapt aspects of Jewish theosophical and ecstatic Kabbalah…with Christ as the focal point and supreme Name for Christian-Cabalist practice”【102:16†source】. Conclusion Forshaw carefully outlines Khunrath’s deep indebtedness to and creative transformation of Reuchlin, Agrippa, and Dee. Rather than passive reception, Khunrath engaged in what Forshaw calls “Neo-Pythagorean speculations on symbolic numbers,” mingling alchemy, theology, and mysticism. “Although Khunrath shows an awareness of Jewish techniques of word permutation, he displays little inclination towards the more complex calculations of ‘literal’ Kabbalah… [He] promotes, like John Dee, the notion of a ‘Cabala of the Real’”【102:7†source】.
